sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Betty, is a 30-year-old female who presents with symptoms of asthma, specifically experiencing daily coughing and wheezing, which she attributes to her asthma.
She reports that these symptoms occur almost every month, with exacerbations requiring frequent visits to urgent care and primary care.
She has been prescribed medications, including a blue inhaler (ventolin/albuterol) and another inhaler that she does not find effective.
She has a history of an asthma exacerbation in the past, which required a hospital visit at age 8, but none since then.
She also mentions that she has a cat that triggers her asthma symptoms, but has avoided her aunt's house since then.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, with frequent exacerbations requiring urgent care and primary care visits.
She has been prescribed medications for her asthma, including a blue inhaler (ventolin/albuterol) and another inhaler that she does not find effective.
She has a history of an asthma exacerbation in the past, which required a hospital visit at age 8, but none since then.
Review of systems: The patient reports no seasonal allergies.
Current medications: Ventolin/albuterol 2 puffs every 4-6 hours p.r.n.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam: Diminished lung sounds throughout, with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
Strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing on fingertips, brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms, one puff twice per day, to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for rescue inhaler use.
I will also put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
She will continue to use Ventolin/albuterol 2 puffs every 4-6 hours p.r.n. for acute exacerbations.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in one month or sooner if she experiences worsening symptoms.
